item.title: Trump announces new effort to escort ships out of Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump on Sunday announced Project Freedom, an effort to escort ships not involved in the war with Iran out of the Strait of Hormuz, will begin Monday.

item.title: Press gala shooting suspect moved off suicide watch, records show
item.description: In a motion requesting Allen's removal from suicide watch, his lawyers said that the restrictions amount to "violations of his rights under the Due Process Clause."

item.title: Man arrested at Trump Miami golf club for disturbance, Secret Service says
item.description: The incident occurred one week after shots were fired during the White House Correspondents' Dinner in Washington, D.C., and Mr. Trump was rushed off the dais.

item.title: Rudy Giuliani hospitalized in critical condition, spokesperson says
item.description: There was no immediate reason given for the 81-year-old's hospitalization.

item.title: Historian Jill Lepore on constitutional amendments | 60 Minutes
item.description: As the U.S. prepares to mark the 250th anniversary of American independence, historian Jill Lepore says now is a good time to ask if the public wants to see constitutional amendments.

item.title: Anderson Cooper learns the thrills of birding
item.description: Anderson Cooper discovers how rare birds, expert guides, and wild landscapes can turn a skeptic into a passionate birder in the mountains of Colombia.

item.title: Inside Grasse, where flowers for Chanel No. 5 have been grown for more than a century
item.description: Centuries ago, the French town of Grasse reeked of pungent odors from leather tanning. But now it's famous for quite the opposite scent from the acres of jasmine that it grows for top perfume houses.

item.title: How natural disasters are exploited to manipulate people online
item.description: John Kelly, head of the social media analytics firm Graphika, showed 60 Minutes correspondent Lesley Stahl how hate groups, foreign governments, and influencers exploit natural disasters to manipulate people on social media.

item.title: Grasse, birthplace of Chanel No. 5, sees perfume revival
item.description: Flower fields once blanketed Grasse, France, where blossoms used in Chanel No. 5 are grown. Now luxury brands are reinvesting in the town long known as the perfume capital of the world.

item.title: Birding takes flight after peace deal between Colombia and guerrilla group | 60 Minutes
item.description: In Colombia, decades of fighting between the government, left-wing guerrillas, right-wing paramilitaries, and narco-traffickers preserved bird habitats in Colombia.

item.title: Discover the town behind the world's most famous perfume
item.description: Centuries ago, the French town of Grasse reeked of pungent odors from leather tanning. But now it's famous for quite the opposite scent from the acres of jasmine that it grows for top perfume houses.

item.title: When the volunteer helping after a hurricane is a white nationalist | 60 Minutes
item.description: After natural disasters, white nationalists, militias, and conspiracists often arrive, offering help. But they also want to recruit and improve their image.

item.title: See some of the stunning birds that call Colombia home
item.description: There are roughly 11,000 bird species around the world and some 2,000 of them can be found in Colombia, a country with diverse geography.

item.title: In Colombia, Anderson Cooper discovers the surprising thrills of birding
item.description: Anderson Cooper discovers how rare birds, expert guides, and wild landscapes can turn a skeptic into a passionate birder in the mountains of Colombia.

item.title: Former guerrillas learn about birding in Colombia
item.description: Years after he was kidnapped by guerrillas in Colombia, a bird expert decided to introduce his former captors to birding, thinking they might make good guides.

item.title: Birds flourish in Colombian region once home to guerrilla groups
item.description: In Colombia, decades of fighting between the government, left-wing guerrillas, right-wing paramilitaries, and narco-traffickers preserved bird habitats in Colombia.

item.title: How natural disasters are exploited to manipulate people online
item.description: John Kelly, head of the social media analytics firm Graphika, showed 60 Minutes correspondent Lesley Stahl how hate groups, foreign governments, and influencers exploit natural disasters to manipulate people on social media.

item.title: Why some white nationalists are showing up to areas hit by natural disasters
item.description: After natural disasters, white nationalists, militias, and conspiracists often arrive, offering help. But they also want to recruit and improve their image.

item.title: A garden with 40 types of trees, shrubs and flowers... in a New York City lobby
item.description: Stopping to smell the roses does not just help you slow down and appreciate life, studies show it can also improve your mental and physical state. Michael George let's us in on a secret green space in Midtown Manhattan.

item.title: Unpacking the decline of Florida's citrus industry
item.description: The fruit that was once synonymous with the sunshine state is slowly disappearing. Cristian Benavides reports on the uphill battle against disease, development and natural disasters that Florida citrus farmers are facing.

item.title: 2 U.S. service members missing amid training exercise in Morocco
item.description: The service members were participating in African Lion, the largest joint military exercise on the continent.

item.title: Transcript: Sen. Raphael Warnock on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," May 3, 2026
item.description: The following is the transcript of the interview with Sen. Raphael Warnock, Democrat of Georgia, that aired on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" on May 3, 2026.

item.title: Sen. Raphael Warnock: Supreme Court "poured fuel on this redistricting arms race"
item.description: Democratic Sen. Raphael Warnock of Georgia called the Supreme Court's decision last week to strike down Louisiana's congressional map and weaken the Voting Rights Act "a massive and devastating blow."

item.title: Watch a Spirit pilot's impromptu retirement celebration after airline shutters
item.description: A Spirit pilot received an impromptu retirement party from a different airline after what would have been his final flight was canceled.

item.title: Ukraine hits Russian oil assets, including "shadow fleet" tankers
item.description: Ukraine has launched a wave of strikes against Russia's oil export infrastructure, including tankers in its "shadow fleet."

item.title: Chevron CEO says it's "very hard to predict" the Iran war's lasting impact on energy prices
item.description: In an interview on April 23, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth told "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" that it's "very hard to predict" the Iran war's lasting impact on energy prices "because markets are dynamic. They can respond to things that we don't anticipate."

item.title: Rep. Jason Crow says U.S. is "not good at having off ramps" in Middle East
item.description: Democratic Rep. Jason Crow of Colorado said on Sunday that the U.S should be considering a broader question of strategy in the war with Iran.

item.title: Ford recalls more than 179,000 vehicles over front seat defect
item.description: Ford Motor Company is recalling over 179,000 vehicles due to a front seat issue that can increase the risk of injury in a crash, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration said.

item.title: ICE reports 18th detainee death in 2026, putting agency on track for new record
item.description: ICE reported the 18th death of an individual in its custody so far this year, putting the agency on track to record a new all-time high in detainee deaths.

item.title: Trump administration to cut 5,000 U.S. troops from Germany
item.description: President Trump said earlier this week he was reviewing the possible reduction of troops in Germany, which hosts more than 35,000 U.S. service members.
